If D ∪Φ |= q and D ∪Φ |= q′ then D ∪Φ |= q ∧q′.
If q |= q′ and D ∪Φ |= q then D ∪Φ |= q′.

If D ⇒C D′ and D ∪Φ |= q then D′ ∪Φ |= q, where C denotes the set of constants that occur in q. Proof. Properties 1 and 2 are trivial. We only show the third one. Let us assume D ⇒C D′ and D ∪Φ |= q. Our task is to show that D′ ∪Φ |= q. Let A be a structure from Φ such that A |= D′. Then we have D′ ⊆I(A). Since D ⇒C D′, there is an injective homomorphism h : D →D′ such that h(c) = c for all c ∈C.
